title: Beyond Faster R-CNN
concept: Mask R-CNN extends Faster R-CNN by adding a parallel branch for predicting pixel-level segmentation masks on each Region of Interest.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main upgrade introduced in Attention U-Net?

options.text: Attention gates that filter out background noise in skip connections
Replacing all convolutions with dense classifier layers
Removing the decoder path to increase execution speed

exercises.explain: Correct! Attention gates filter features to focus on targets.

cards.title: Adding a brush to a box
cards.body: Faster R-CNN is a famous object detector. It finds objects and outputs boxes and labels. But what if we want the exact mask of each object, not just a box? Instead of starting from scratch, the creators of Mask R-CNN made a simple but genius move: they added a third branch to Faster R-CNN.

cards.title: Mask R-CNN
cards.body: Mask R-CNN is an instance segmentation model. It builds on Faster R-CNN by adding a parallel branch that predicts a binary mask (pixel-level outline) for each Region of Interest (RoI), running alongside the classification and box regression branches.

cards.title: A painter following a detector
cards.body: Imagine a supervisor (Faster R-CNN) drawing boxes on a canvas. In Mask R-CNN, we hire an artist (the Mask Branch) who walks up to each box and carefully paints the object inside it, ignoring everything outside.

cards.title: Three outputs
cards.body:
For a photo of a dog, Mask R-CNN outputs:
1. Class label: 'Dog'
2. Bounding box coordinates
3. A detailed pixel mask tracing the dog's exact shape.

cards.title: Not a sequential step
cards.body: You might think the model detects the box first, crops the image, and then runs a separate model. In reality, the mask branch runs **in parallel** with the box and class branches, sharing the same feature backbone for speed.

cards.title: Mask R-CNN Architecture
cards.body: How the branches run in parallel:

left.text:
Branch 1: Class Predictor (is it a dog?)
Branch 2: Box Regressor (where is the box?)

right.text:
Branch 3: Mask Predictor (which pixels are the dog?)
Runs in parallel, sharing backbone features

exercises.prompt: What branch does Mask R-CNN add to Faster R-CNN?

options.text: A parallel branch for predicting pixel-level masks
A branch for converting images to grayscale
A flattening branch for dense classification
A downsampling branch for pooling features

exercises.explain: Correct! Mask R-CNN adds a parallel mask prediction branch.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The mask branch in Mask R-CNN runs in sequence after the bounding box is fully drawn.
exercises.explain: False. The mask branch runs in parallel with the box and classification branches.

exercises.sentence: Mask R-CNN is designed for ___ segmentation, locating and outlining individual objects.
exercises.explain: Mask R-CNN is a classic instance segmentation model.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs is unique to Mask R-CNN compared to Faster R-CNN?

options.text: A pixel-level binary mask for each object

exercises.explain: The binary mask is the key extension of Mask R-CNN.

exercises.prompt: Why is running the mask branch in parallel more efficient than running a separate segmentation network after detection?

options.text: It allows the branches to share feature maps extracted by the backbone, avoiding duplicate convolutions.
It reduces the resolution of the input images to save space.
It eliminates the need for a backbone network entirely.
It allows the model to train without backpropagation.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Shared features are the key to deep learning efficiency. By sharing the backbone, Mask R-CNN adds segmentation with very little extra compute. Next: how do we align pixels perfectly?
